## Cyberpunk 2077 suddenly stopped running
[...]
- Author: [@philipl](https://github.com/philipl)
- State: closed (completed)
- Created: 2025-11-28T20:20:14Z
- Updated: 2025-11-29T00:24:02Z
- Closed: 2025-11-29T00:24:02Z
- Closed by: [@philipl](https://github.com/philipl)
[...]
I don't have much confidence that this is a VKD3D-Proton problem but I want to ask.
[...]
Today, Cyberpunk suddenly stopped running, and consistently crashes inside the nvidia driver. Everything worked yesterday, and I have verified that no relevant system level packages updates took place since the last time I ran the game. This is the same nvidia driver, kernel, mesa, etc that worked yesterday.
[...]
- Completely reinstalled the game with a clean prefix
- Tried hiding the nvidia gpu
- Disabled all vulkan layers except the ones bundled with the nvidia driver
- Tried Proton experimental, 10-3 and GE-Proton10-25. All with the same results.
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 - Patch 2.31
[...]
- GPU: nvidia 5090
- Driver: 580.105.08
- Wine version: Proton Experimental 2025-11-26 (but also 10-3 and GE-Proton10-25)
- VKD3D-Proton version: [v2.14.1](https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/releases/tag/v2.14.1)-472-g[32bffb836cb0](https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/commits/32bffb836cb0)
[...]
** commented ·
[...]

The game is 100% stable during normal gameplay both with and without the descriptor heap support enabled; the crash is specific to pressing spacebar to capture a photo in photo mode with heap support enabled.

## nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs
[...]
- Author: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
- State: closed (completed)
- Labels: bug
- Created: 2026-04-26T03:58:43Z
- Updated: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed by: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
[...]
On a hybrid graphics laptop, Vulkan device creation (vkCreateDevice) fails on the NVIDIA dGPU when using the NVIDIA open kernel driver, preventing D3D12 workloads via VKD3D-Proton from running.
[...]
This manifests as DX12 games (e.g. Cyberpunk 2077) failing to initialize on the NVIDIA GPU and falling back to the AMD iGPU, or failing outright when fallback is disabled.
[...]
- Occurs only with nvidia-open
- Does not occur with the proprietary NVIDIA driver (same version)
- Does not occur on the AMD iGPU (RADV)
- DX11 games via DXVK work correctly on the NVIDIA GPU
- Native Vulkan applications also work
[...]
The failure happens during Vulkan device creation, before any D3D12 pipelines or shaders are created.
[...]
1. Use a hybrid graphics laptop (AMD iGPU + NVIDIA dGPU)
2. Install nvidia-open driver (595.58.03)
3. Use Proton / GE-Proton / Proton-CachyOS (Optionally, I tested it with all of these versions & Proton-CachyOS + with VKD3D-Proton (latest upstream git))
4. Launch a DX12-only game, e.g. Cyberpunk 2077
5. Observe Vulkan device creation failure on NVIDIA GPU
[...]
- Set VKD3D_NO_FALLBACK=1 to prevent fallback to iGPU
- Game fails immediately during initialization OR Game launches but only utilizes iGPU.
[...]
Expected behavior:

- Vulkan device creation should succeed on the NVIDIA dGPU, allowing D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton) to run, as it does with the proprietary driver
Source: nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs · Issue #1123 · NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules (https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123)

it seems it does not really relate

9800X3D, 64GB RAM, Nvidia 3090.

content.text:
# Cyberpunk 2077 Linux Freezing Issues: Summary

## Key Problems Identified

Multiple freeze and crash issues affect Cyberpunk 2077 on Linux with NVIDIA GPUs, primarily related to the Vulkan/VKD3D stack:

### 1. **VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST Crashes (NVIDIA 580.x drivers)**
- **Symptom:** Game crashes after 5–45 minutes of gameplay with `VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST` error
- **Cause:** Related to "intense view map pressure" and descriptor heap memory exhaustion (5120+ views)
- **Affected:** NVIDIA RTX 2080 and newer, Proton Experimental and GE-Proton
- **Workarounds attempted (all eventually fail):**
  - `VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv` (best result, ~45 min before crash)
  - `VKD3D_FEATURE_LEVEL=12_0`
  - `PROTON_HEAP_DELAY_FREE=1`
  - Disabling ray tracing and lowering graphics settings
- **Status:** Unresolved; no kernel errors reported—issue appears at Vulkan/userspace level

### 2. **System-Wide Freeze on Launch (nvidia-open driver)**
- **Symptom:** Complete system freeze during opening logo animation; mouse/keyboard unresponsive
- **Cause:** Appears to be a driver/compositor interaction issue (IO_PAGE_FAULT, flip event timeout, GSP timeout in kernel logs)
- **Affected:** nvidia-open driver (not proprietary driver)
- **Workaround:** Very inconsistent; one user reported it working after switching to Proton-Experimental Bleeding-Edge

### 3. **Photo Mode Crash with GPU Descriptor Heap**
- **Symptom:** `VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST` when taking photos in photo mode
- **Trigger:** Specific to `PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1` environment variable
- **Status:** Game stable during normal gameplay; crash only on photo capture

### 4. **Hybrid GPU Issues**
- **nvidia-open + AMD iGPU:** Vulkan device creation fails on NVIDIA dGPU; falls back to AMD iGPU or fails entirely
  - Does **not** occur with proprietary NVIDIA driver
  - Workaround: Hide iGPU in BIOS or upgrade Mesa (e.g., 25.2.6 → 25.2.7 resolved one case)

### 5. **Gamescope Hangs (every few minutes)**
- **Symptom:** Audio continues while graphics freeze for 5–10 seconds
- **Affected:** gamescope 3.16.20 with NVIDIA RTX 5080, HDR enabled
- **Status:** Unresolved despite multiple environment variable workarounds

## Recommended Actions

1. **If using nvidia-open:** Consider switching to the proprietary NVIDIA driver (confirmed working in comparison tests)
2. **For VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST:** Update NVIDIA driver and Proton/VKD3D-Proton to latest versions; avoid `PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1`
3. **For hybrid laptops (nvidia-open + AMD iGPU):** Use proprietary driver or upgrade Mesa to latest
4. **If using gamescope:** Avoid HDR flags or wait for gamescope update (3.16.17+ with KDE Plasma 6.5.3 compatibility)

## Important Caveat

**No definitive universal fix exists.** Issues span multiple components (NVIDIA drivers, VKD3D-Proton, gamescope, Proton) with overlapping but distinct failure modes. Individual system configurations (kernel version, driver, compositor) heavily influence reproduction and workaround effectiveness.

---

## Sources

1. Games running in gamescope hang every few minutes — https://github.com/ValveSoftware/gamescope/issues/2096
2. Launching Cyberpunk 2077 completely locks up the system (nvidia-open) — https://www.answeroverflow.com/m/1446960445473816646
3. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756
4. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST in photo mode with GPU descriptor heap — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2979
5. nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12 (hybrid GPUs) — https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123
6. Cyberpunk 2077 fails to start with nvidia dGPU + AMD iGPU — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2698

`[Desktop Entry]
 Name=Steam Big Picture (Gamescope)
 Comment=Steam Big Picture mode with Gamescope compositor
 Exec=env GAMESCOPE_WSI_HIDE_PRESENT_WAIT_EXT=1 VKD3D_DISABLE_EXTENSIONS=VK_KHR_present_wait STEAM_GAMESCOPE_SESSION=1 gamescope -W 3840 -H 2160 -r 120 -e -f -O HDMI-A-2
 --prefer-vk-device 10de:2c02 --hdr-enabled --hdr-itm-enable --hdr-itm-target-nits 650 --hdr-sdr-content-nits 200 -- steam -gamepadui
 Type=Application`

run in hdr

reason for using

## [Cyberpunk 2077] VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 crashes consistently with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST on NVIDIA 580.x driver after 5-45 minutes of gameplay. The crash appears related to descriptor heap pressure ("Intense view map pressure" in logs). Hardware is confirmed stable via stress testing. Multiple workarounds attempted without success.
[...]
- Arch Linux, kernel 6.17.9-zen
- NVIDIA RTX 2080, driver 580.105.08
- Ryzen 9 9950X3D, 96GB RAM
- Proton Experimental / GE-Proton (both affected)
[...]
Game freezes with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after variable playtime (5-45 minutes depending on settings). GPU utilization drops from 99% to 1%.
[...]
shows

Intense view map pressure, recreating view maps (2048 -> 3072)
vkd3d_queue_wait_idle: vkQueueWaitIdle failed, vr -4 (VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST)
[...]
### Tested workarounds (all still crash eventually):
[...]
- VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv (best result, ~45min before crash)
- VKD3D_FEATURE_LEVEL=12_0
- PROTON_HEAP_DELAY_FREE=1
- PROTON_ENABLE_NVAPI=0
- Ray Tracing OFF, lowest graphics settings
- Driver downgrade failed (DKMS incompatible with kernel 6.17).
[...]
**GPU stress test**: gpu-burn 5 minutes, 0 errors - hardware is stable.
[...]
> > Intense view map pressure
> 
> This is completely benign. And 3k is nothing here. It's expected when RT is enabled.
[...]
> Looking at the logs more closely, the crash happens ~14 seconds after "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views". Between the warning and the crash, there's no other error - just QueryInterface warnings which seem unrelated.
> 
> Timeline:
> 
> - 1773.845: "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views"
> - 1788.417: "Failed to submit queue(s), vr -4" → VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST
> 
> This was with RT OFF and lowest settings. Is 5120 views still considered normal?
> 
> I can provide dmesg/kernel logs from the crash if that would help identify the driver-side failure.
[...]
> Additional findings from further testing:
> 
> ### No kernel errors
> 
> No kernel/dmesg errors during the crash. The GPU device lost happens purely at Vulkan/userspace level - the NVIDIA kernel driver (580.105.08) doesn't report any fault or timeout.
> 
> ### System-wide freeze
> 
> The freeze affects the entire desktop, not just the game process. Mouse/keyboard become unresponsive fo
Source: [Cyberpunk 2077] VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x · Issue #2756 · HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton (https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756)

## HDR broken using gamescope 3.16.17 on KDE Plasma 6.5.3 on Arch Linux
[...]
The HDR functionality of gamescope broke because of an update of KDE Plasma on Arch Linux.

The current version of gamescope that is delivered on Arch Linux is gamescope 3.16.17-1 updated on 2025-09-08.
The current version of KDE Plasma on Arch Linux is 6.5.3, updated on 2025-12-09.

There was a change in the code of KDE Plasma that handles HDR some time in the last two months. Since then the old version of gamescope (2025-09-08) is not compatible anymore to use the HDR functionality.

The same developer that changed the code in Plasma also made gamescope compatible again by this commit: https://github.com/ValveSoftware/gamescope/commit/7d4e835a25cc85017380cbd743d517b48932fb75
[...]
Unfortunately there was no update from gamescope since 2025-09-08, so Arch Linux users now have a broken HDR functionality until gamescope releases a new update or the package on the Arch Repos gets a hotfix update to include the commit mentioned above manually.
[...]
A rollback of KDE Plasma to something like 6.5.0 is no viable option to many users as it affects many packages and dependencies. A rollback of gamescope is useless, as the incompatibility didn't come from the change in gamescope, but a change in KDE Plasma.
[...]
1) Start a Wayland Session of KDE Plasma 6.5.3 on Arch Linux
2) Install the newest gamescope version
3) Try to use the --hdr-enabled flag when starting gamescope
[...]
--> HDR does not work. It displays SDR and settings menus in games show HDR als "unavailable".
[...]
```markdown
- Distro: Arch Linux x86_64
- CPU: AMD Ryzen 9 5900X 12-Core Processor
- GPU: Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. [AMD/ATI] Navi 21 [Radeon RX 6800/6800 XT / 6900 XT]
- Driver Version: Mesa 25.3.1-arch1.2
```
[...]
```markdown
- Desktop environment: KDE Plasma 6.5.3
- Session type: Wayland
- Gamescope version: 3.16.17
- Gamescope launch command(s), generated by Lutris, starting Cyberpunk 2077, GOG version:
[...]
gamescope -w 3840 -h 2160 -W 3840 -H 2160 -f --hdr-enabled --force-grab-cursor -- gamemoderun /home/user/.local/share/lutris/runners/wine/wine-ge-8-26-x86_64/bin/wine /home/user/games/gog/cyberpunk-2077/drive_c/GOG Games/Cyberpunk 2077/bin/x64/Cyberpunk2077.exe
```
Source: HDR broken using gamescope 3.16.17 on KDE Plasma 6.5.3 on Arch Linux · Issue #2037 · ValveSoftware/gamescope (https://github.com/ValveSoftware/gamescope/issues/2037)

This fork extends [Valve’s gamescope](https://github.com/ValveSoftware/gamescope) with fixes for **NVIDIA proprietary drivers** on Linux, with emphasis on **RTX 40/50 (Blackwell)** and **driver 570+**, especially **hybrid laptops** (NVIDIA dGPU + integrated GPU running the desktop compositor).
[...]
**Branch with the NVIDIA work:** `feat/nvidia-blackwell-explicit-sync`
[...]
### What the NVIDIA patches change
[...]
- **Nested Wayland + NVIDIA:** If `/dev/nvidiactl` exists and you run under a Wayland session with `--backend auto` (default), gamescope selects the **SDL** backend instead of the Wayland backend. The Wayland backend shares output via DMA-BUF to the parent compositor, which often **fails on hybrid NVIDIA setups** (invalid `wl_buffer` / broken pipe).
[...]
- **Vulkan:** Detects `VK_DRIVER_ID_NVIDIA_PROPRIETARY`, skips Mesa-only paths (e.g. certain WSI memory import), and applies safer defaults for flippable images and sync.
[...]
- **DRM / KMS:** Disables explicit sync by default on NVIDIA (implicit sync fallback); optionally enables an experimental **SYNC_FD** path via ConVar `drm_nvidia_explicit_sync_via_sync_fd`. Proactively disables `IN_FENCE_FD` where it causes atomic commit failures.
[...]
- **Wayland backend:** If you force `--backend wayland`, DMA-BUF modifiers are checked before `create_immed` to avoid fatal protocol errors; explicit sync is not advertised for NVIDIA clients the same way as on Mesa.
[...]
- **Preemptive upscale / timelines:** NVIDIA-specific semaphore bridging and audited error handling (no silent GPU sync skips, FD leaks fixed).
[...]
**Tested by the maintainer:** RTX 5050 Laptop GPU, driver 570, Pop!_OS 24.04 (Wayland), including real games via Steam/Proton.
[...]
**Not a guarantee** for every NVIDIA GPU, every driver version, or nouveau. Reports with `nvidia-smi`, distro, and desktop help narrow issues.
[...]
In an embedded session, gamescope can flip game frames with DRM/KMS with minimal copying. When nested on a normal desktop:
Source: barramee27/gamescope (https://github.com/barramee27/gamescope)

## nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs
[...]
- Author: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
- State: closed (completed)
- Labels: bug
- Created: 2026-04-26T03:58:43Z
- Updated: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed by: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
[...]
On a hybrid graphics laptop, Vulkan device creation (vkCreateDevice) fails on the NVIDIA dGPU when using the NVIDIA open kernel driver, preventing D3D12 workloads via VKD3D-Proton from running.
[...]
This manifests as DX12 games (e.g. Cyberpunk 2077) failing to initialize on the NVIDIA GPU and falling back to the AMD iGPU, or failing outright when fallback is disabled.
[...]
- Occurs only with nvidia-open
- Does not occur with the proprietary NVIDIA driver (same version)
- Does not occur on the AMD iGPU (RADV)
- DX11 games via DXVK work correctly on the NVIDIA GPU
- Native Vulkan applications also work
[...]
The failure happens during Vulkan device creation, before any D3D12 pipelines or shaders are created.
[...]
1. Use a hybrid graphics laptop (AMD iGPU + NVIDIA dGPU)
2. Install nvidia-open driver (595.58.03)
3. Use Proton / GE-Proton / Proton-CachyOS (Optionally, I tested it with all of these versions & Proton-CachyOS + with VKD3D-Proton (latest upstream git))
4. Launch a DX12-only game, e.g. Cyberpunk 2077
5. Observe Vulkan device creation failure on NVIDIA GPU
[...]
- Set VKD3D_NO_FALLBACK=1 to prevent fallback to iGPU
- Game fails immediately during initialization OR Game launches but only utilizes iGPU.
[...]
Expected behavior:

- Vulkan device creation should succeed on the NVIDIA dGPU, allowing D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton) to run, as it does with the proprietary driver
Source: nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs · Issue #1123 · NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules (https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123)

Which you can view after a reboot by looking at the previous boot's log.
[...]
Oh boy, looking at the last boot:

## nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs
[...]
595.58.03 (packaged nvidia-open from CachyOS repositories (no local patches))
[...]
On a hybrid graphics laptop, Vulkan device creation (vkCreateDevice) fails on the NVIDIA dGPU when using the NVIDIA open kernel driver, preventing D3D12 workloads via VKD3D-Proton from running.
[...]
This manifests as DX12 games (e.g. Cyberpunk 2077) failing to initialize on the NVIDIA GPU and falling back to the AMD iGPU, or failing outright when fallback is disabled.
[...]
- Occurs only with nvidia-open
- Does not occur with the proprietary NVIDIA driver (same version)
- Does not occur on the AMD iGPU (RADV)
- DX11 games via DXVK work correctly on the NVIDIA GPU
- Native Vulkan applications also work
[...]
The failure happens during Vulkan device creation, before any D3D12 pipelines or shaders are created.
[...]
1. Use a hybrid graphics laptop (AMD iGPU + NVIDIA dGPU)
2. Install nvidia-open driver (595.58.03)
3. Use Proton / GE-Proton / Proton-CachyOS (Optionally, I tested it with all of these versions & Proton-CachyOS + with VKD3D-Proton (latest upstream git))
4. Launch a DX12-only game, e.g. Cyberpunk 2077
5. Observe Vulkan device creation failure on NVIDIA GPU
[...]
- Set VKD3D_NO_FALLBACK=1 to prevent fallback to iGPU
- Game fails immediately during initialization OR Game launches but only utilizes iGPU.
[...]
Expected behavior:

- Vulkan device creation should succeed on the NVIDIA dGPU, allowing D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton) to run, as it does with the proprietary driver
Source: nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs · Issue #1123 · NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules (https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123)

## Cyberpunk 2077 suddenly stopped running
[...]
Today, Cyberpunk suddenly stopped running, and consistently crashes inside the nvidia driver. Everything worked yesterday, and I have verified that no relevant system level packages updates took place since the last time I ran the game. This is the same nvidia driver, kernel, mesa, etc that worked yesterday.
[...]
- Completely reinstalled the game with a clean prefix
- Tried hiding the nvidia gpu
- Disabled all vulkan layers except the ones bundled with the nvidia driver
- Tried Proton experimental, 10-3 and GE-Proton10-25. All with the same results.
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 - Patch 2.31
[...]
- GPU: nvidia 5090
- Driver: 580.105.08
- Wine version: Proton Experimental 2025-11-26 (but also 10-3 and GE-Proton10-25)
- VKD3D-Proton version: [v2.14.1](https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/releases/tag/v2.14.1)-472-g[32bffb836cb0](https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/commits/32bffb836cb0)
[...]
> Well, I tried out Proton-Experimental Bleeding-Edge and it worked again. Maybe the 2025-11-26 Proton Experimental wasn't installed until after I last played the game yesterday or something like that. I will report on the Proton issues.
Source: Cyberpunk 2077 suddenly stopped running · Issue #2728 · HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton (https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2728)

summary.text:
# Cyberpunk 2077 Linux Freezing Issues: Summary

## Key Problems Identified

Multiple freeze and crash issues affect Cyberpunk 2077 on Linux with NVIDIA GPUs, primarily related to the Vulkan/VKD3D stack:

### 1. **VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST Crashes (NVIDIA 580.x drivers)**
- **Symptom:** Game crashes after 5–45 minutes of gameplay with `VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST` error
- **Cause:** Related to "intense view map pressure" and descriptor heap memory exhaustion (5120+ views)
- **Affected:** NVIDIA RTX 2080 and newer, Proton Experimental and GE-Proton
- **Workarounds attempted (all eventually fail):**
  - `VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv` (best result, ~45 min before crash)
  - `VKD3D_FEATURE_LEVEL=12_0`
  - `PROTON_HEAP_DELAY_FREE=1`
  - Disabling ray tracing and lowering graphics settings
- **Status:** Unresolved; no kernel errors reported—issue appears at Vulkan/userspace level

### 2. **System-Wide Freeze on Launch (nvidia-open driver)**
- **Symptom:** Complete system freeze during opening logo animation; mouse/keyboard unresponsive
- **Cause:** Appears to be a driver/compositor interaction issue (IO_PAGE_FAULT, flip event timeout, GSP timeout in kernel logs)
- **Affected:** nvidia-open driver (not proprietary driver)
- **Workaround:** Very inconsistent; one user reported it working after switching to Proton-Experimental Bleeding-Edge

### 3. **Photo Mode Crash with GPU Descriptor Heap**
- **Symptom:** `VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST` when taking photos in photo mode
- **Trigger:** Specific to `PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1` environment variable
- **Status:** Game stable during normal gameplay; crash only on photo capture

### 4. **Hybrid GPU Issues**
- **nvidia-open + AMD iGPU:** Vulkan device creation fails on NVIDIA dGPU; falls back to AMD iGPU or fails entirely
  - Does **not** occur with proprietary NVIDIA driver
  - Workaround: Hide iGPU in BIOS or upgrade Mesa (e.g., 25.2.6 → 25.2.7 resolved one case)

### 5. **Gamescope Hangs (every few minutes)**
- **Symptom:** Audio continues while graphics freeze for 5–10 seconds
- **Affected:** gamescope 3.16.20 with NVIDIA RTX 5080, HDR enabled
- **Status:** Unresolved despite multiple environment variable workarounds

## Recommended Actions

1. **If using nvidia-open:** Consider switching to the proprietary NVIDIA driver (confirmed working in comparison tests)
2. **For VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST:** Update NVIDIA driver and Proton/VKD3D-Proton to latest versions; avoid `PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1`
3. **For hybrid laptops (nvidia-open + AMD iGPU):** Use proprietary driver or upgrade Mesa to latest
4. **If using gamescope:** Avoid HDR flags or wait for gamescope update (3.16.17+ with KDE Plasma 6.5.3 compatibility)

## Important Caveat

**No definitive universal fix exists.** Issues span multiple components (NVIDIA drivers, VKD3D-Proton, gamescope, Proton) with overlapping but distinct failure modes. Individual system configurations (kernel version, driver, compositor) heavily influence reproduction and workaround effectiveness.

---

## Sources

1. Games running in gamescope hang every few minutes — https://github.com/ValveSoftware/gamescope/issues/2096
2. Launching Cyberpunk 2077 completely locks up the system (nvidia-open) — https://www.answeroverflow.com/m/1446960445473816646
3. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756
4. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST in photo mode with GPU descriptor heap — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2979
5. nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12 (hybrid GPUs) — https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123
6. Cyberpunk 2077 fails to start with nvidia dGPU + AMD iGPU — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2698

content.thinking:
Key findings:
1. The user is on **nvidia-open** driver (nvidia-open-dkms)
2. There's a specific note that **nvidia-open** has issues, and switching to the **proprietary** driver has been confirmed working in comparison tests
3. The freeze pattern (works first time, freezes on second map/inventory open) matches the view map recreation pressure issue
4. Gamescope also has hanging issues with NVIDIA
5. `VKD3D_SHADER_CACHE_PATH=0` didn't help

The crucial detail I missed: the user is on **nvidia-open-dkms** (the open kernel module), NOT the proprietary driver. The answer overflow link mentions that launching Cyberpunk completely locks up the system with nvidia-open.

Let me also consider:
- The user is on Wayland
- RTX 2070 SUPER (Turing, not Ada/Blackwell)
- Driver 595.71.05

The nvidia-open driver has known issues with VKD3D, particularly with Vulkan descriptor management. Switching to the proprietary nvidia-dkms package might be the actual fix.

Let me also check if there's a VKD3D version issue - GE-Proton10-34 might ship an older VKD3D that doesn't have recent fixes.

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the research more carefully:

> **nvidia-open + AMD iGPU:** Vulkan device creation fails on NVIDIA dGPU; falls back to AMD iGPU or fails entirely
> Does **not** occur with proprietary NVIDIA driver

And:

> **If using nvidia-open:** Consider switching to the proprietary NVIDIA driver (confirmed working in comparison tests)

This is significant. The user has nvidia-open-dkms installed. Let me present this as the most likely root cause and the recommended path forward.

But also, I should also present the immediate workarounds since switching drivers is a bigger step.

Let me also check one more thing - `VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv` was mentioned as the best (though still imperfect) workaround for the view map pressure issue. The user didn't trythat yet.

content.text: Here's what I missed — you're running **`nvidia-open-dkms`**, not the proprietary driver. This is likely the root cause.

content.text:
i2c_nvidia_gpu         16384  0
nvidia_drm            151552  99
nvidia_modeset       2195456  22 nvidia_drm

video                  81920  2 asus_wmi,nvidia_modeset

---
filename:       /lib/modules/7.0.9-arch2-1/updates/dkms/nvidia.ko.zst
import_ns:      DMA_BUF
alias:          char-major-195-*
description:    NVIDIA core GPU kernel module
version:        595.71.05

OK so the open kernel module is loaded and active. Here's where we stand:

## The Real Problem: `nvidia-open-dkms`

You're on the **open kernel module** (`nvidia-open-dkms`). There are multiple confirmed reports that VKD3D-Proton has descriptor heap and Vulkan device management bugs **specifically with nvidia-open** that don't exist with the proprietary driver. The freeze-on-second-overlay-open pattern maps directly to this.

The shader cache fix didn't work because your freeze isn't the cache bug — it's the **view map recreation** path in VKD3D losing the device when the open driver's Vulkan implementation tries to resize descriptor tables.

## Two paths forward:

### Option A — Switch to `nvidia-dkms` (proprietary kernel module)

This is the actual fix. Multiple users on the same bug tracker confirmed that switching from `nvidia-open` to proprietary resolved their freezes entirely.

```bash
# Remove open, install proprietary
sudo pacman -R nvidia-open-dkms nvidia-hook
sudo pacman -S nvidia-dkms

# Rebuild initramfs
sudo mkinitcpio -P

# Reboot
reboot
```

Your launch options stay minimal:
```
VKD3D_CONFIG=nodxr gamemoderun %command% --launcher-skip
```

The `nvidia-dkms` package provides the same driver version (595.71.05) but with the closed-source kernel module that has proper Vulkan descriptor heap support.

### Option B — Try workarounds without switching drivers (less likely to help)

```
VKD3D_CONFIG=nodxr,no_upload_hvv DXVK_NVAPI_DISABLE_LOW_LATENCY=1 gamemoderun %command% --launcher-skip
```

`no_upload_hvv` is the best of the VKD3D env vars for the view map pressure bug — it defers certain host-visible uploads. It might extend your playtime but reports say it still eventually crashes.

---

**My recommendation:** Switch to `nvidia-dkms`. The open module's Vulkan stack is the weak link here — it's the common factor across all these VKD3D freezes. The proprietary module handles the descriptor heap paths correctly.

Want me to walk through the driver swap step by step?
Okay we can try that

Arch Linux - News: NVIDIA 590 driver drops Pascal and lower support; main packages switch to Open Kernel Modules

## NVIDIA 590 driver drops Pascal and lower support; main packages switch to Open Kernel Modules

With the update to driver version 590, the NVIDIA driver no longer supports Pascal (GTX 10xx) GPUs or older. We will replace the`nvidia` package with`nvidia-open`,`nvidia-dkms` with`nvidia-open-dkms`, and`nvidia-lts` with`nvidia-lts-open`.

Impact: Updating the NVIDIA packages on systems with Pascal, Maxwell, or older cards will fail to load the driver, which may result in a broken graphical environment.

Intervention required for Pascal/older users: Users with GTX 10xx series and older cards must switch to the legacy proprietary branch to maintain support:

- Uninstall the official`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms` packages.
- Install`nvidia-580xx-dkms` from the AUR

Users with Turing (20xx and GTX 1650 series) and newer GPUs will automatically transition to the open kernel modules on upgrade and require no manual intervention.

The Arch Linux name and logo are recognized trademarks. Some rights reserved.

Install the Driver Package: Install the NVIDIA driver and utilities using pacman:
[...]
```
$ sudo pacman -S nvidia nvidia-utils nvidia-settings
```
[...]
This installs:
[...]
- `nvidia`– The proprietary NVIDIA kernel modules for the standard linux kernel
- `nvidia-utils`– NVIDIA userspace utilities and libraries
- `nvidia-settings`– GUI configuration tool for NVIDIA graphics
[...]
Note: If you’re using the linux-lts kernel, replace`nvidia` with`nvidia-lts`:
[...]
LINUX LTS KERNEL Arch Linux offers both the standard rolling-release kernel (linux) and a Long Term Support kernel (linux-lts) for users who prefer stability over cutting-edge features. To check which kernel you’re currently using, run:`uname -r`. If the output contains “lts” (e.g., 6.6.x-lts), you’re using the LTS kernel and should install nvidia-lts instead of nvidia.
[...]
```
$ sudo pacman -S nvidia-lts nvidia-utils nvidia-settings
```
[...]
different driver packages:
[...]
- `nvidia-open` or`nvidia-open-lts`– Open-source kernel modules for Turing and newer GPUs (RTX 2000 series and above)
- `nvidia-470xx-dkms`– For older Kepler GPUs (GTX 600-900 series)
- `nvidia-390xx-dkms`– For even older Fermi GPUs (GTX 400-500 series)
Source: Arch Linux: NVIDIA Drivers Installation - LinuxConfig (https://linuxconfig.org/arch-linux-nvidia-drivers-installation)

Then in May 2022, NVIDIA flipped the switch. The company published its Linux GPU kernel modules as open source under a dual GPL/MIT license, starting with the R515 driver release. That decision set off a chain of architectural changes that is still playing out today, culminating in the R590 series dropping support for Pascal and older hardware entirely and making the open kernel module the default across Arch Linux, Ubuntu, and every major distribution that has followed suit.
[...]
NVIDIA's May 2022 announcement was more surgical than a full open-sourcing. The company published the kernel modules -- the code that runs inside the Linux kernel space -- under a dual GPL/MIT license. The user-space components of the driver, including the OpenGL and Vulkan libraries, remained closed-source binaries. This distinction matters and has since drawn criticism from parts of the community.
[...]
The open kernel modules shipped in two "flavors," a term NVIDIA uses in its documentation. The proprietary flavor is the historical default -- the one NVIDIA has shipped since its earliest Linux drivers. The open flavor is the newly published source, dual-lic
Source: NVIDIA Linux Drivers: Open Modules, GSP Firmware, and the Road to Blackwell - sudowheel.com (https://sudowheel.com/nvidia-linux-drivers.html)

content.text:
# NVIDIA Driver Packages on Arch Linux

## Key Changes (2026)

**Package Consolidation:** Arch Linux has transitioned from proprietary to open-source kernel modules:
- `nvidia` → `nvidia-open` (mainline kernel)
- `nvidia-dkms` → `nvidia-open-dkms` (non-mainline kernels)
- `nvidia-lts` → `nvidia-lts-open`

This change occurred with the NVIDIA 590 driver release, which dropped support for Pascal (GTX 10xx) and older GPUs.

## Package Selection Guide

### For Turing (RTX 20xx) and Newer GPUs
- **Mainline kernel (`linux`)**: Install `nvidia-open`
- **Non-mainline kernels** (`linux-lts`, `linux-zen`, etc.): Install `nvidia-open-dkms` + kernel headers

### For Older GPUs (Pascal and Earlier)
Users must switch to the AUR-hosted proprietary driver:
- **GTX 10xx (Pascal)**: `nvidia-580xx-dkms` from AUR
- **GTX 600-900 (Kepler)**: `nvidia-470xx-dkms` from AUR
- **GTX 400-500 (Fermi)**: `nvidia-390xx-dkms` from AUR

## Installation Optimization

**ArchInstall (as of April 2026)** now intelligently selects packages:
- When all selected kernels are mainline, it installs `nvidia-open` directly (faster, fewer dependencies)
- For non-mainline kernels, it switches to `nvidia-open-dkms` + `dkms` + kernel headers as needed

## Caveats

- **GSP Firmware issues**: The open modules may cause power management problems on Turing GPUs and complete failures on some Ampere laptops. Affected users should use `nvidia-580xx-dkms` with the kernel parameter `NVreg_EnableGpuFirmware=0`.
- **Legacy package confusion**: Some tools still reference the deprecated `nvidia-dkms` package, which no longer exists in official repos.

## Sources

1. https://archlinux.org/news/nvidia-590-driver-drops-pascal-support-main-packages-switch-to-open-kernel-modules/
2. https://github.com/archlinux/archinstall/pull/4347
3. https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA
4. https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia-open/
5. https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia-open-dkms/

- Author: [@dylanmtaylor](https://github.com/dylanmtaylor)

- SHA: a3c6bd5d4582acfd62b27f169bb46b2c28aa36fb
- Changes: +16 -6 (2 files)
- Verified: yes
[...]
```diff
@@ -43,6 +43,7 @@ class GfxPackage(Enum):
 	LibvaIntelDriver = 'libva-intel-driver'
 	LibvaNvidiaDriver = 'libva-nvidia-driver'
 	Mesa = 'mesa'
+	NvidiaOpen = 'nvidia-open'
 	NvidiaOpenDkms = 'nvidia-open-dkms'
 	VulkanIntel = 'vulkan-intel'
 	VulkanRadeon = 'vulkan-radeon'
```
[...]
@@ -222,13 +222,22 @@ def install_greeter(self, install_session: Installer, greeter: GreeterType) -> N
 	def install_gfx_driver(self, install_session: Installer, driver: GfxDriver) -> None:
 		debug(f'Installing GFX driver: {driver.value}')
 
-		if driver in [GfxDriver.NvidiaOpenKernel]:
-			headers = [f'{kernel}-headers' for kernel in install_session.kernels]
-			# Fixes https://github.com/archlinux/archinstall/issues/585
-			install_session.add_additional_packages(headers)
-
 		driver_pkgs = driver.gfx_packages()
 		pkg_names = [p.value for p in driver_pkgs]
+
+		# For Nvidia open kernel modules, use nvidia-open instead of nvidia-open-dkms
+		# when all selected kernels are mainline (no dkms needed). This avoids
+		# installing dkms + kernel headers and speeds up installation.
+		if driver == GfxDriver.NvidiaOpenKernel:
+			needs_dkms = any('-' in k for k in install_session.kernels)
+
+			if needs_dkms:
+				headers = [f'{kernel}-headers' for kernel in install_session.kernels]
+				install_session.add_additional_packages(headers)
+			else:
+				pkg_names = [GfxPackage.NvidiaOpen.value if p == GfxPackage.NvidiaOpenDkms.value else p for p in pkg_names]
+				pkg_names = [p for p in pkg_names if p != GfxPackage.Dkms.value]
+
 		install_session.add_additional_packages(pkg_names)
 
 	def install_profile_config(self, install_session: Installer, profile_config: ProfileConfiguration) -> None:

Install the Driver Package: Install the NVIDIA driver and utilities using pacman:
[...]
```
$ sudo pacman -S nvidia nvidia-utils nvidia-settings
```
[...]
This installs:
[...]
- `nvidia`– The proprietary NVIDIA kernel modules for the standard linux kernel
- `nvidia-utils`– NVIDIA userspace utilities and libraries
- `nvidia-settings`– GUI configuration tool for NVIDIA graphics
[...]
Note: If you’re using the linux-lts kernel, replace`nvidia` with`nvidia-lts`:
[...]
LINUX LTS KERNEL Arch Linux offers both the standard rolling-release kernel (linux) and a Long Term Support kernel (linux-lts) for users who prefer stability over cutting-edge features. To check which kernel you’re currently using, run:`uname -r`. If the output contains “lts” (e.g., 6.6.x-lts), you’re using the LTS kernel and should install nvidia-lts instead of nvidia.
[...]
```
$ sudo pacman -S nvidia-lts nvidia-utils nvidia-settings
```
[...]
different driver packages:
[...]
- `nvidia-open` or`nvidia-open-lts`– Open-source kernel modules for Turing and newer GPUs (RTX 2000 series and above)
- `nvidia-470xx-dkms`– For older Kepler GPUs (GTX 600-900 series)
- `nvidia-390xx-dkms`– For even older Fermi GPUs (GTX 400-500 series)
Source: Arch Linux: NVIDIA Drivers Installation - LinuxConfig (https://linuxconfig.org/arch-linux-nvidia-drivers-installation)

Then in May 2022, NVIDIA flipped the switch. The company published its Linux GPU kernel modules as open source under a dual GPL/MIT license, starting with the R515 driver release. That decision set off a chain of architectural changes that is still playing out today, culminating in the R590 series dropping support for Pascal and older hardware entirely and making the open kernel module the default across Arch Linux, Ubuntu, and every major distribution that has followed suit.
[...]
NVIDIA's May 2022 announcement was more surgical than a full open-sourcing. The company published the kernel modules -- the code that runs inside the Linux kernel space -- under a dual GPL/MIT license. The user-space components of the driver, including the OpenGL and Vulkan libraries, remained closed-source binaries. This distinction matters and has since drawn criticism from parts of the community.
[...]
The open kernel modules shipped in two "flavors," a term NVIDIA uses in its documentation. The proprietary flavor is the historical default -- the one NVIDIA has shipped since its earliest Linux drivers. The open flavor is the newly published source, dual-lic

summary.text:
# NVIDIA Driver Packages on Arch Linux

## Key Changes (2026)

**Package Consolidation:** Arch Linux has transitioned from proprietary to open-source kernel modules:
- `nvidia` → `nvidia-open` (mainline kernel)
- `nvidia-dkms` → `nvidia-open-dkms` (non-mainline kernels)
- `nvidia-lts` → `nvidia-lts-open`

This change occurred with the NVIDIA 590 driver release, which dropped support for Pascal (GTX 10xx) and older GPUs.

## Package Selection Guide

### For Turing (RTX 20xx) and Newer GPUs
- **Mainline kernel (`linux`)**: Install `nvidia-open`
- **Non-mainline kernels** (`linux-lts`, `linux-zen`, etc.): Install `nvidia-open-dkms` + kernel headers

### For Older GPUs (Pascal and Earlier)
Users must switch to the AUR-hosted proprietary driver:
- **GTX 10xx (Pascal)**: `nvidia-580xx-dkms` from AUR
- **GTX 600-900 (Kepler)**: `nvidia-470xx-dkms` from AUR
- **GTX 400-500 (Fermi)**: `nvidia-390xx-dkms` from AUR

## Installation Optimization

**ArchInstall (as of April 2026)** now intelligently selects packages:
- When all selected kernels are mainline, it installs `nvidia-open` directly (faster, fewer dependencies)
- For non-mainline kernels, it switches to `nvidia-open-dkms` + `dkms` + kernel headers as needed

## Caveats

- **GSP Firmware issues**: The open modules may cause power management problems on Turing GPUs and complete failures on some Ampere laptops. Affected users should use `nvidia-580xx-dkms` with the kernel parameter `NVreg_EnableGpuFirmware=0`.
- **Legacy package confusion**: Some tools still reference the deprecated `nvidia-dkms` package, which no longer exists in official repos.

## Sources

1. https://archlinux.org/news/nvidia-590-driver-drops-pascal-support-main-packages-switch-to-open-kernel-modules/
2. https://github.com/archlinux/archinstall/pull/4347
3. https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA
4. https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia-open/
5. https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia-open-dkms/

content.thinking:
OK, so Arch has completely dropped the proprietary `nvidia-dkms` package. Since the 590 driver release, the mainline packages are all open kernel modules. The only way to get the proprietary driver is through AUR (`nvidia-580xx-dkms`) which is for Pascal and older — but the user has an RTX 2070 SUPER (Turing), so that won't work either because the 580xx is the last proprietary driver supporting Pascal.

Wait, let me re-read. The 590 driver dropped Pascal support. But for Turing and newer, the official packages are now open-only. The proprietary driver still exists but might need to be installed differently.

Let me check if there's a way to install the proprietary NVIDIA driver for Turing GPUs on Arch.

Actually, the key info is:
- `nvidia` → renamed to `nvidia-open` (mainline kernel package)
- `nvidia-dkms` → renamed to `nvidia-open-dkms` (for non-mainline kernels)
- The proprietary versions no longer exist in official repos

So there's no straightforward way to switch to proprietary. The AUR option `nvidia-580xx-dkms` is for older GPUs (Pascal) and only has driver version 580.x.

But wait — the user's GPU is RTX 2070 SUPER (Turing) which is supported by the 595 driver. The 580xx legacy driver wouldn't support it properly.

So the option of switching to proprietary isn't really available through normal means on Arch anymore. The open driver IS the official driver now.

Let me think about other approaches:
1. Downgrade NVIDIA driver — might not help
2. Use AUR proprietary build — would need some hackery
3. Try other workarounds

Actually let me look more carefully. Is there an AUR package for the current proprietary NVIDIAdriver?

content.text: Well, that changes things. Arch has **dropped the proprietary NVIDIA driver from official repos** — the open kernel module is now the only option in the regular repos.

The proprietary NVIDIA graphics card driver does not need any Xorg server configuration file. You can start X to see if the Xorg server will function correctly without a configuration file. However, it may be required to create a configuration file (prefer`/etc/X11/xorg.conf.d/20-nvidia.conf` over`/etc/X11/xorg.conf`) in order to adjust various settings. This configuration can be generated by the NVIDIA Xorg configuration tool, or it can be created manually. Arch Linux is making a major change to how it handles NVIDIA graphics drivers. With the release of driver version 590, the Arch Linux distribution is officially moving to Open Kernel Modules.
[...]
Arch Linux is replacing its standard proprietary driver packages with open-source versions. This means the software that helps your graphics card talk to your computer is now more transparent. Specifically, the system will swap these packages:
[...]
- `nvidia` becomes`nvidia-open`.
- `nvidia-dkms` becomes`nvidia-open-dkms`.
- `nvidia-lts` becomes`nvidia-lts-open`.
[...]
In addition to these name changes, the new driver drops support for older hardware. Therefore, you must identify your graphics card model before you update your system.
[...]
If you own a modern graphics card, you have nothing to worry about. Users with Turing (RTX 20xx / GTX 1650) or newer GPUs will transition to the new modules automatically. Your system will handle the switch during a normal update, and you do not need to perform any manual tasks.
[...]
The situation is different for users with older hardware. NVIDIA driver 590 no longer supports Pascal (GTX 10xx) GPUs or any older cards. This includes Maxwell and older architectures.
[...]
If you have a GTX 10xx series card or older, you must switch to a legacy driver branch to stay supported. Follow these simple steps to keep your system running:
[...]
1. Uninstall your current official NVIDIA packages, such as`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms`.
2. Install the legacy driver from the Arch User Repository (AUR) using Paru or Yay helpers.
3. Search for and install the package named`nvidia-580xx-dkms`.
[...]
Some users confirmed that you don't need to uninstall anything. Just find out the installed nvidia packages using command:
[...]
Then install those packages from AUR using Paru or Yay like below:
[...]
```
paru -Syu nvidia-580xx-dkms nvidia-58

Arch Linux is making a major change to how it handles NVIDIA graphics drivers. With the release of driver version 590, the Arch Linux distribution is officially moving to Open Kernel Modules.
[...]
Arch Linux is replacing its standard proprietary driver packages with open-source versions. This means the software that helps your graphics card talk to your computer is now more transparent. Specifically, the system will swap these packages:
[...]
- `nvidia` becomes`nvidia-open`.
- `nvidia-dkms` becomes`nvidia-open-dkms`.
- `nvidia-lts` becomes`nvidia-lts-open`.

## How to Update Arch Linux NVIDIA Drivers and Fix Pascal GPU Support
[...]
If you have a GTX 10xx series card or older, you must switch to a legacy driver branch to stay supported. Follow these simple steps to keep your system running:

1. Uninstall your current official NVIDIA packages, such as`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms`.
2. Install the legacy driver from the Arch User Repository (AUR) using Paru or Yay helpers.
3. Search for and install the package named`nvidia-580xx-dkms`.

With`20250613.12fe085f-5`, we split our firmware into several vendor-focused packages.`linux-firmware` is now an empty package depending on our default set of firmware.
[...]
Unfortunately, this coincided with upstream reorganizing the symlink layout of the NVIDIA firmware, resulting in a situation that Pacman cannot handle. When attempting to upgrade from`20250508.788aadc8-2` or earlier, you will see the following errors:

```

```
[...]
To progress with the system upgrade, first remove`linux-firmware`, then reinstall it as part of the upgrade:
[...]
```
# pacman -Rdd linux-firmware
# pacman -Syu linux-firmware

```
[...]
On the AUR, a good search term for people affected by this change is`580xx`. Link: https://aur.archlinux.org/packages?O=0&K=580xx

You may also do`yay -Ss 580xx` or`paru -Ss 580xx` in your terminal.

Source: NVIDIA GPU users, attention please! (Dec-2025) - Important notifications - EndeavourOS (https://forum.endeavouros.com/t/nvidia-gpu-users-attention-please-dec-2025/77119?page=2)

Dropping “real” support for older GPUs is usually a routine headline… right up until it strands thousands of users at a black screen after a simple`pacman -Syu`. That’s essentially what’s been happening with NVIDIA’s latest driver transition on Arch Linux: a mix of branch changes, packaging decisions, and a lack of runway has caused systems with Pascal (GeForce GTX 10xx) or Maxwell (GTX 9xx) GPUs to go from a working desktop to a setup where the proprietary module won’t load — and the machine boots straight into the CLI.
[...]
The trigger is Arch moving to the 590 driver branch (for example, 590.48.01-1), plus the broader shift toward the nvidia-open stack for newer hardware. In practical terms: Turing (GTX 16 / RTX 20) and newer stay supported, while Maxwell and Pascal fall off the main path and need a “legacy” route if you want to keep using NVIDIA’s proprietary driver.
[...]
Arch is different by design: fast updates are the point. That’s great when your hardware remains inside the supported window — and painful when support ends at a specific driver version. After an update, you may end up with a driver that no longer recognizes your GPU, so Xorg/Wayland fails or the module never loads.
[...]
On top of that, Arch’s push toward`nvidia-open`(NVIDIA’s “open” kernel modules — not Nouveau) adds another source of confusion: this isn’t just “a new driver,” it’s often a new set of packages and a new default path for supported GPUs.
[...]
| Architecture | Typical series | Examples | Status after the change | What to do |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Maxwell | GTX 9xx | 960, 970, 980 | Dropped from the main supported branch | Use a legacy proprietary branch / switch to an open driver / upgrade GPU |
| Pascal | GTX 10xx | 1050 Ti, 1060, 1070, 1080 | Dropped from the main supported branch | Legacy branch (580xx) or migration strategy |
| Turing+ | GTX 16xx / RTX 20xx+ | 1660, 2060, 3060, 4070… | Still supported | Follow Arch’s package migration guidance (often toward`nvidia-open`) |
Source: NVIDIA Drops Pascal Support on Linux — and Arch Linux Users Feel the Blast Radius: What Happened and How Not to Lose SSH + Your Desktop - System Administration (https://systemadministration.net/nvidia-drops-pascal-support-on-linux-and-arch-linux-users-feel-the-blast-radius-what-happened-and-how-not-to-lose-ssh-your-desktop/)

queries.answer:
## [Cyberpunk 2077] VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 crashes consistently with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST on NVIDIA 580.x driver after 5-45 minutes of gameplay. The crash appears related to descriptor heap pressure ("Intense view map pressure" in logs). Hardware is confirmed stable via stress testing. Multiple workarounds attempted without success.
[...]
- Arch Linux, kernel 6.17.9-zen
- NVIDIA RTX 2080, driver 580.105.08
- Ryzen 9 9950X3D, 96GB RAM
- Proton Experimental / GE-Proton (both affected)
[...]
Game freezes with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after variable playtime (5-45 minutes depending on settings). GPU utilization drops from 99% to 1%.
[...]
Intense view map pressure, recreating view maps (2048 -> 3072)
vkd3d_queue_wait_idle: vkQueueWaitIdle failed, vr -4 (VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST)
[...]
### Tested workarounds (all still crash eventually):
[...]
- VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv (best result, ~45min before crash)
- VKD3D_FEATURE_LEVEL=12_0
- PROTON_HEAP_DELAY_FREE=1
- PROTON_ENABLE_NVAPI=0
- Ray Tracing OFF, lowest graphics settings
- Driver downgrade failed (DKMS incompatible with kernel 6.17).
[...]
**GPU stress test**: gpu-burn 5 minutes, 0 errors - hardware is stable.
[...]
> > Intense view map pressure
> 
> This is completely benign. And 3k is nothing here. It's expected when RT is enabled.
[...]
> Looking at the logs more closely, the crash happens ~14 seconds after "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views". Between the warning and the crash, there's no other error - just QueryInterface warnings which seem unrelated.
> 
> Timeline:
> 
> - 1773.845: "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views"
> - 1788.417: "Failed to submit queue(s), vr -4" → VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST
> 
> This was with RT OFF and lowest settings. Is 5120 views still considered normal?
> 
> I can provide dmesg/kernel logs from the crash if that would help identify the driver-side failure.
[...]
> Additional findings from further testing:
> 
> ### No kernel errors
> 
> No kernel/dmesg errors during the crash. The GPU device lost happens purely at Vulkan/userspace level - the NVIDIA kernel driver (580.105.08) doesn't report any fault or timeout.
> 
> ### System-wide freeze
> 
> The freeze affects the entire desktop, not just the game process. Mouse/keyboard become unresponsive fo
Source: [Cyberpunk 2077] VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x · Issue #2756 · HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton (https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756)

## VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST in Cyberpunk 2077 photo mode with GPU descriptor heap enabled
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 hangs with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST when taking a photo in photo mode while GPU descriptor heap support is enabled via PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1. The game is 100% stable during normal gameplay both with and without the descriptor heap support enabled; the crash is specific to pressing spacebar to capture a photo in photo mode with heap support enabled.
[...]
| Component | Details |
| --- | --- |
| **CPU** | AMD Ryzen 9 9950X 16-Core (32 threads) |
| **GPU** | NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 |
| **Driver** | NVIDIA 595.71.05 |
| **OS** | CachyOS |
| **Kernel** | 7.0.2-2-cachyos (x86_64) |
[...]
The crash reproduces across multiple proton-cachyos releases & vkd3d-proton builds, including:
[...]
- cachyos-10.0-20260420-slr / Proton Experimental 10.0-20260407 / vkd3d-proton 2889f1c8
- cachyos-11.0-20260428-slr / Proton Experimental 11.0-20260428 / vkd3d-proton 790cfc68
[...]
1. Install Cyberpunk 2077
2. Use proton-cachyos (tested with `cachyos-10.0-20260420-slr` and `cachyos-11.0-20260428-slr`)
3. Set launch options / environment: `PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1`
4. Launch game, load a save, enter photo mode
5. Press spacebar to take a photo
6. Graphics hang immediately — display freezes, audio continues playing
[...]
0aca10
[...]
Maybe related: shortly before the crash, the log shows repeated `ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint` calls with heap invalidation warnings:
[...]
```
87018.032: d3d12_command_list_ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint: ClearUAVUint called without active heap.
87018.032: d3d12_command_list_invalidate_descriptor_heap: Invalidating descriptor heap due to meta command which is incompatible with heaps.
```
[...]
Then the hang:
[...]
```
87018.180: d3d12_device_mark_as_removed: Device 0000000129330080 is lost (reason 0x887a0005, "VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST").
87018.180: vkd3d_memory_transfer_queue_flush_locked: Failed to submit command buffer, vr -4.
87018.180: d3d12_device_mark_as_removed: Device 0000000129330080 is lost (reason 0x80004005, "Failed to execute pending memory clears.").
87018.181: d3d1
Source: VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST in Cyberpunk 2077 photo mode with GPU descriptor heap enabled · Issue #2979 · HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton (https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2979)

## nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs
[...]
Author: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov
[...]
- State: closed (completed)
- Labels: bug
- Created: 2026-04-26T03:58:43Z
- Updated: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed by: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
[...]
On a hybrid graphics laptop, Vulkan device creation (vkCreateDevice) fails on the NVIDIA dGPU when using the NVIDIA open kernel driver, preventing D3D12 workloads via VKD3D-Proton from running.
[...]
This manifests as DX12 games (e.g. Cyberpunk 2077) failing to initialize on the NVIDIA GPU and falling back to the AMD iGPU, or failing outright when fallback is disabled.
[...]
- Occurs only with nvidia-open
- Does not occur with the proprietary NVIDIA driver (same version)
- Does not occur on the AMD iGPU (RADV)
- DX11 games via DXVK work correctly on the NVIDIA GPU
- Native Vulkan applications also work

The failure happens during Vulkan device creation, before any D3D12 pipelines or shaders are created.
[...]
1. Use a hybrid graphics laptop (AMD iGPU + NVIDIA dGPU)
2. Install nvidia-open driver (595.58.03)
3. Use Proton / GE-Proton / Proton-CachyOS (Optionally, I tested it with all of these versions & Proton-CachyOS + with VKD3D-Proton (latest upstream git))
4. Launch a DX12-only game, e.g. Cyberpunk 2077
5. Observe Vulkan device creation failure on NVIDIA GPU
[...]
- Set VKD3D_NO_FALLBACK=1 to prevent fallback to iGPU
- Game fails immediately during initialization OR Game launches but only utilizes iGPU.
[...]
Expected behavior:

- Vulkan device creation should succeed on the NVIDIA dGPU, allowing D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton) to run, as it does with the proprietary driver
Source: nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs · Issue #1123 · NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules (https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123)

Any hints on what this could be, because it seems it does not really relate to proton itself...
[...]
9800X3D, 64GB RAM, Nvidia 3090.
[...]
When you can switch to a different tty (called "virtual consoles"), can you run a system resource monitor tool like `top` to see what might be choking the system?

The last time I could switch to a different TTY, the most ressource intensive thing was the game itself, but the CPU was nowhere near fully utilized, and RAM was still plenty free. I'll have to try a few times, because sometimes the lock is too bad and I have to kill the PC.

Next thing I can suggest is looking at `dmesg` output (the kernel log).

Which you can view after a reboot by looking at the previous boot's log.

Oh boy, looking at the last boot:

(the last bit could be related to the hard shutdown)

content.text:
# NVIDIA Driver Support on Arch Linux: RTX 2070 (Turing) & Driver Choice

## Key Findings

### Hardware Support Status
**RTX 2070 (Turing architecture) is supported** by both:
- `nvidia-open` (open kernel modules) — **Arch's new default**
- `nvidia-580xx-dkms` (proprietary driver) — **Available from AUR**

### Arch Linux's Driver Transition (Driver 590+)
Arch moved to NVIDIA driver 590 with a major shift in package handling:
- Turing (RTX 20xx) and newer → transitioned to **`nvidia-open`** (open kernel modules)
- Pascal (GTX 10xx) and older → dropped from main repos, legacy drivers pushed to AUR
- The package `nvidia` was replaced with `nvidia-open`; `nvidia-dkms` with `nvidia-open-dkms`

### Installing Proprietary Driver Instead of nvidia-open
If you prefer the proprietary driver despite Arch's default:

1. **Uninstall open modules**: Remove `nvidia-open`, `nvidia-open-dkms`, or similar
2. **Install from AUR**: Use `paru` or `yay` to install `nvidia-580xx-dkms` and related utilities:
   ```
   paru -Syu nvidia-580xx-dkms nvidia-580xx-utils lib32-nvidia-580xx-utils
   ```
3. **Optional**: Search AUR for `580xx` to find matching packages (settings, OpenCL, etc.)

No uninstall is strictly required if you simply install the AUR packages on top.

## Known Issues & Caveats

### nvidia-open Specific Issues
- **Hybrid laptop GPUs**: `nvidia-open` fails to create Vulkan devices for D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton), while the proprietary driver works fine
- **GSP Firmware problems**: Can cause poor power management on Turing GPUs and complete failures on Ampere laptops; workaround: use proprietary driver with `NVreg_EnableGpuFirmware=0`
- **Linux 5.18+ with Intel 11th Gen CPUs**: Indirect Branch Tracking (IBT) incompatibility; disable with `ibt=off` kernel parameter (security tradeoff)

### Gaming/VKD3D-Proton Issues (Both Drivers)
- Multiple reports of **VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST crashes** in Cyberpunk 2077 on both NVIDIA 580.x and 595.x drivers
- Crashes appear related to descriptor heap pressure and view map recreation
- Workarounds tested without full success; newer Proton versions sometimes resolve issues

## Current Driver Versions (2026)
- Latest `nvidia-open`: **595.58.03** (as of April 2026)
- Legacy proprietary (`nvidia-580xx-dkms`): **580.119.02** available from AUR

## Sources
1. ArchWiki NVIDIA — https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA
2. NVIDIA Driver 590 Drops Pascal Support: Arch Linux Update Guide — https://ostechnix.com/nvidia-590-driver-arch-linux-manual-intervention/
3. Arch Linux nvidia-open package page — https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia/
4. nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails on hybrid laptops — https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123
5. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST Cyberpunk 2077 issues — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756

curatedQueries.answer:
Supported both by:
[...]
- nvidia-open with no RTD3 Power Management on Turing2, possible crashes on Ampere-equiped laptops2
- nvidia-580xx-dkms AUR
[...]
1. If these packages do not work (usually due to new hardware releases) nvidia-open-beta AUR may have a newer version that offers support.
2. NVIDIA's GSP firmware is known to cause issues, from suboptimal power management of Turing GPUs to complete failure on some laptops containing Ampere GPUs. If affected, use the proprietary driver (e.g. nvidia-580xx-dkms AUR) with the module parameter`NVreg_EnableGpuFirmware=0` instead.
3. May not function correctly on Linux 5.18 (or later) on systems with Intel CPUs 11th Gen and newer due an incompatibility with Indirect Branch Tracking. You can disable it by setting the`ibt=off` kernel parameter from the boot loader. Be aware, this security feature is responsible for mitigating a class of exploit techniques.
[...]
4. NVIDIA no longer actively supports these cards and their drivers may not officially support the current Xorg version. It might be easier to use the nouveau driver; however, NVIDIA's legacy drivers are still available and might provide better 3D performance/stability.
[...]

[...]
e.g. nvidia-340xx-dkms AUR),
[...]

[...]
the`nvidia-modprobe` utility automatically creates them
[...]
etc/udev/rules.d
[...]
70-nvidia.rules
[...]
to run it automatically:
[...]
The proprietary NVIDIA graphics card driver does not need any Xorg server configuration file. You can start X to see if the Xorg server will function correctly without a configuration file. However, it may be required to create a configuration file (prefer`/etc/X11/xorg.conf.d/20-nvidia.conf` over`/etc/X11/xorg.conf`) in order to adjust various settings. This configuration can be generated by the NVIDIA Xorg configuration tool, or it can be created manually. If created manually, it can be a minimal configuration (in the sense that it will only pass the basic options to the Xorg server), or it can include a number of settings that can bypass Xorg's auto-discovered or pre-configured options.
[...]

Source: NVIDIA - ArchWiki (https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA)

Arch Linux is making a major change to how it handles NVIDIA graphics drivers. With the release of driver version 590, the Arch Linux distribution is officially moving to Open Kernel Modules.
[...]
Arch Linux is replacing its standard proprietary driver packages with open-source versions. This means the software that helps your graphics card talk to your computer is now more transparent. Specifically, the system will swap these packages:
[...]
- `nvidia` becomes`nvidia-open`.
- `nvidia-dkms` becomes`nvidia-open-dkms`.
- `nvidia-lts` becomes`nvidia-lts-open`.
[...]
In addition to these name changes, the new driver drops support for older hardware. Therefore, you must identify your graphics card model before you update your system.
[...]
If you own a modern graphics card, you have nothing to worry about. Users with Turing (RTX 20xx / GTX 1650) or newer GPUs will transition to the new modules automatically. Your system will handle the switch during a normal update, and you do not need to perform any manual tasks.
[...]
The situation is different for users with older hardware. NVIDIA driver 590 no longer supports Pascal (GTX 10xx) GPUs or any older cards. This includes Maxwell and older architectures.
[...]
If you have a GTX 10xx series card or older, you must switch to a legacy driver branch to stay supported. Follow these simple steps to keep your system running:
[...]
1. Uninstall your current official NVIDIA packages, such as`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms`.
2. Install the legacy driver from the Arch User Repository (AUR) using Paru or Yay helpers.
3. Search for and install the package named`nvidia-580xx-dkms`.
[...]
Some users confirmed that you don't need to uninstall anything. Just find out the installed nvidia packages using command:
[...]
Then install those packages from AUR using Paru or Yay like below:
[...]
```
paru -Syu nvidia-580xx-dkms nvidia-58
Source: NVIDIA Driver 590 Drops Pascal Support: Arch Linux Update Guide - OSTechNix (https://ostechnix.com/nvidia-590-driver-arch-linux-manual-intervention/)

But it's not entirely Arch Linux's fault. This stems from NVIDIA dropping Pascal support and Arch's decision to push legacy drivers to AUR.
[...]
The distro recently switched to the NVIDIA 590 driver, which dropped support for Pascal (GTX 10xx) and older GPUs (GTX 900) on Linux. Alongside this, they also transitioned users of NVIDIA 20xx and newer GPUs to open kernel modules.
[...]

[...]
If you have a GTX 10xx, GTX 9xx, or older card, updating breaks your graphics, the new driver fails to load, and you get plopped into the command-line interface(CLI) with no GUI in sight.
[...]
To fix this, the Arch Linux developers recommend that you first uninstall the existing NVIDIA packages:`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms`. Then, install`nvidia-580xx-dkms`, which is a community-maintained package from the Arch User Repository(AUR) that contains the old proprietary driver that works on these GPUs.
[...]
If that doesn't suit you, then you could always switch to nouveau, the open source driver for NVIDIA hardware. But expect weaker performance than what you usually get...
Source: If You Have an Older NVIDIA GPU on Arch Linux, Your System Might Be Broken (https://itsfoss.com/news/arch-linux-old-nvidia-gpu-issue/)

Arch Linux is making a major change to how it handles NVIDIA graphics drivers. With the release of driver version 590, the Arch Linux distribution is officially moving to Open Kernel Modules.
[...]
Arch Linux is replacing its standard proprietary driver packages with open-source versions. This means the software that helps your graphics card talk to your computer is now more transparent. Specifically, the system will swap these packages:
[...]
- `nvidia` becomes`nvidia-open`.
- `nvidia-dkms` becomes`nvidia-open-dkms`.
- `nvidia-lts` becomes`nvidia-lts-open`.
[...]
## How to Update Arch Linux NVIDIA Drivers and Fix Pascal GPU Support
[...]
If you have a GTX 10xx series card or older, you must switch to a legacy driver branch to stay supported. Follow these simple steps to keep your system running:
[...]
1. Uninstall your current official NVIDIA packages, such as`nvidia`,`nvidia-lts`, or`nvidia-dkms`.
2. Install the legacy driver from the Arch User Repository (AUR) using Paru or Yay helpers.
3. Search for and install the package named`nvidia-580xx-dkms`.
[...]
Some users confirmed that you don't need to uninstall anything. Just find out the installed nvidia packages using command:
[...]
```
pacman -Qss nvidia
```
[...]
Note down the installed nvidia packages (E.g. nvidia, lib32-nvidia-utils, nvidia-utils)
[...]
Then install those packages from AUR using Paru or Yay like below:
[...]
```
paru -Syu nvidia-580xx-dkms nvidia-58
Source: NVIDIA Driver 590 Drops Pascal Support: Arch Linux Update Guide - OSTechNix (https://ostechnix.com/nvidia-590-driver-arch-linux-manual-intervention/)

With`20250613.12fe085f-5`, we split our firmware into several vendor-focused packages.`linux-firmware` is now an empty package depending on our default set of firmware.
[...]
Unfortunately, this coincided with upstream reorganizing the symlink layout of the NVIDIA firmware, resulting in a situation that Pacman cannot handle. When attempting to upgrade from`20250508.788aadc8-2` or earlier, you will see the following errors:
[...]
```
linux-firmware-nvidia: /usr/lib/firmware/nvidia/ad103 exists in filesystem
linux-firmware-nvidia: /usr/lib/firmware/nvidia/ad104 exists in filesystem
linux-firmware-nvidia: /usr/lib/firmware/nvidia/ad106 exists in filesystem
linux-firmware-nvidia: /usr/lib/firmware/nvidia/ad107 exists in filesystem

```
[...]
To progress with the system upgrade, first remove`linux-firmware`, then reinstall it as part of the upgrade:
[...]
```
# pacman -Rdd linux-firmware
# pacman -Syu linux-firmware

```
[...]
On the AUR, a good search term for people affected by this change is`580xx`. Link: https://aur.archlinux.org/packages?O=0&K=580xx
[...]
You may also do`yay -Ss 580xx` or`paru -Ss 580xx` in your terminal.
[...]
Couple the above search query with`pacman -Q | grep -A 0 -E "nvidia"` to find out which packages you should be seeking to replace.
[...]
```
pacman -Q | grep -A 0 -E "nvidia"

--
linux-firmware-nvidia 20251125-2
--

nvidia-settings 580.119.02-1 <-- Replace me
nvidia-utils 580.119.02-1 <-- Replace me
--
opencl-nvidia 580.119.02-1 <-- Replace me

```
[...]
```
yay -Ss 580xx
aur/nvidia-580xx-settings 580.119.02-2 (+3 2.99) 
    Tool for configuring the NVIDIA graphics driver (580xx)
aur/lib32-opencl-nvidia-580xx 580.119.02-1 (+5 4.97) 
    OpenCL implemention for NVIDIA (32-bit) (580xx)
aur/lib32-nvidia-580xx-utils 580.119.02-1 (+5 4.97) 
    NVIDIA drivers utilities (32-bit) (580xx)
[...]
aur/libxnvctrl-580xx 580.119.02-2 (+3 2.99) 
    NVIDIA NV-CONTROL X extension (580xx)
aur/opencl-nvidia-580xx 580.119.02-2 (+14 13.86) 
    OpenCL implemention for NVIDIA (580xx)
aur/nvidia-580xx-utils 580.119.02-2 (+14 13.86) 
    NVIDIA drivers utilities (580xx)
[...]
aur/nvidia-580xx-dkms 580.119.02-2 (+14 13.86) 
    NVIDIA kernel modules - module source
Source: NVIDIA GPU users, attention please! (Dec-2025) - Important notifications - EndeavourOS (https://forum.endeavouros.com/t/nvidia-gpu-users-attention-please-dec-2025/77119?page=2)

Dropping “real” support for older GPUs is usually a routine headline… right up until it strands thousands of users at a black screen after a simple`pacman -Syu`. That’s essentially what’s been happening with NVIDIA’s latest driver transition on Arch Linux: a mix of branch changes, packaging decisions, and a lack of runway has caused systems with Pascal (GeForce GTX 10xx) or Maxwell (GTX 9xx) GPUs to go from a working desktop to a setup where the proprietary module won’t load — and the machine boots straight into the CLI.
[...]
The trigger is Arch moving to the 590 driver branch (for example, 590.48.01-1), plus the broader shift toward the nvidia-open stack for newer hardware. In practical terms: Turing (GTX 16 / RTX 20) and newer stay supported, while Maxwell and Pascal fall off the main path and need a “legacy” route if you want to keep using NVIDIA’s proprietary driver.
[...]
Arch is different by design: fast updates are the point. That’s great when your hardware remains inside the supported window — and painful when support ends at a specific driver version. After an update, you may end up with a driver that no longer recognizes your GPU, so Xorg/Wayland fails or the module never loads.
[...]
On top of that, Arch’s push toward`nvidia-open`(NVIDIA’s “open” kernel modules — not Nouveau) adds another source of confusion: this isn’t just “a new driver,” it’s often a new set of packages and a new default path for supported GPUs.
[...]
| Architecture | Typical series | Examples | Status after the change | What to do |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Maxwell | GTX 9xx | 960, 970, 980 | Dropped from the main supported branch | Use a legacy proprietary branch / switch to an open driver / upgrade GPU |
| Pascal | GTX 10xx | 1050 Ti, 1060, 1070, 1080 | Dropped from the main supported branch | Legacy branch (580xx) or migration strategy |
| Turing+ | GTX 16xx / RTX 20xx+ | 1660, 2060, 3060, 4070… | Still supported | Follow Arch’s package migration guidance (often toward`nvidia-open`) |
Source: NVIDIA Drops Pascal Support on Linux — and Arch Linux Users Feel the Blast Radius: What Happened and How Not to Lose SSH + Your Desktop - System Administration (https://systemadministration.net/nvidia-drops-pascal-support-on-linux-and-arch-linux-users-feel-the-blast-radius-what-happened-and-how-not-to-lose-ssh-your-desktop/)

## [Cyberpunk 2077] VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after view map pressure on NVIDIA 580.x
[...]
Cyberpunk 2077 crashes consistently with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST on NVIDIA 580.x driver after 5-45 minutes of gameplay. The crash appears related to descriptor heap pressure ("Intense view map pressure" in logs). Hardware is confirmed stable via stress testing. Multiple workarounds attempted without success.
[...]
- Arch Linux, kernel 6.17.9-zen
- NVIDIA RTX 2080, driver 580.105.08
- Ryzen 9 9950X3D, 96GB RAM
- Proton Experimental / GE-Proton (both affected)
[...]
Game freezes with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST after variable playtime (5-45 minutes depending on settings). GPU utilization drops from 99% to 1%.
[...]
Intense view map pressure, recreating view maps (2048 -> 3072)
vkd3d_queue_wait_idle: vkQueueWaitIdle failed, vr -4 (VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST)
[...]
### Tested workarounds (all still crash eventually):
[...]
- VKD3D_CONFIG=no_upload_hvv (best result, ~45min before crash)
- VKD3D_FEATURE_LEVEL=12_0
- PROTON_HEAP_DELAY_FREE=1
- PROTON_ENABLE_NVAPI=0
- Ray Tracing OFF, lowest graphics settings
- Driver downgrade failed (DKMS incompatible with kernel 6.17).
[...]
**GPU stress test**: gpu-burn 5 minutes, 0 errors - hardware is stable.
[...]
> > Intense view map pressure
> 
> This is completely benign. And 3k is nothing here. It's expected when RT is enabled.
[...]
> Looking at the logs more closely, the crash happens ~14 seconds after "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views". Between the warning and the crash, there's no other error - just QueryInterface warnings which seem unrelated.
> 
> Timeline:
> 
> - 1773.845: "Intense view map pressure! Got 5120 views"
> - 1788.417: "Failed to submit queue(s), vr -4" → VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST
> 
> This was with RT OFF and lowest settings. Is 5120 views still considered normal?
> 
> I can provide dmesg/kernel logs from the crash if that would help identify the driver-side failure.
[...]
> Additional findings from further testing:
> 
> ### No kernel errors
> 
> No kernel/dmesg errors during the crash. The GPU device lost happens purely at Vulkan/userspace level - the NVIDIA kernel driver (580.105.08) doesn't report any fault or timeout.
> 
> ### System-wide freeze
> 
> The freeze affects the entire desktop, not just the game process. Mouse/keyboard become unresponsive fo

Cyberpunk 2077 hangs with VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST when taking a photo in photo mode while GPU descriptor heap support is enabled via PROTON_VKD3D_HEAP=1. The game is 100% stable during normal gameplay both with and without the descriptor heap support enabled; the crash is specific to pressing spacebar to capture a photo in photo mode with heap support enabled.
[...]

| **CPU** | AMD Ryzen 9 9950X 16-Core (32 threads) |
| **GPU** | NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 |
| **Driver** | NVIDIA 595.71.05 |
| **OS** | CachyOS |
| **Kernel** | 7.0.2-2-cachyos (x86_64) |

The crash reproduces across multiple proton-cachyos releases & vkd3d-proton builds, including:

- cachyos-11.0-20260428-slr / Proton Experimental 11.0-20260428 / vkd3d-proton 790cfc68

4. Launch game, load a save, enter photo mode

Then the hang:

## nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails for D3D12-class Vulkan workloads (VKD3D-Proton) on hybrid laptop GPUs
[...]
Author: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov
[...]
- State: closed (completed)
- Labels: bug
- Created: 2026-04-26T03:58:43Z
- Updated: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed: 2026-05-14T22:42:09Z
- Closed by: [@adiyat-abubakirov](https://github.com/adiyat-abubakirov)
[...]
On a hybrid graphics laptop, Vulkan device creation (vkCreateDevice) fails on the NVIDIA dGPU when using the NVIDIA open kernel driver, preventing D3D12 workloads via VKD3D-Proton from running.
[...]
This manifests as DX12 games (e.g. Cyberpunk 2077) failing to initialize on the NVIDIA GPU and falling back to the AMD iGPU, or failing outright when fallback is disabled.
[...]
- Occurs only with nvidia-open
- Does not occur with the proprietary NVIDIA driver (same version)
- Does not occur on the AMD iGPU (RADV)
- DX11 games via DXVK work correctly on the NVIDIA GPU
- Native Vulkan applications also work

The failure happens during Vulkan device creation, before any D3D12 pipelines or shaders are created.
[...]
1. Use a hybrid graphics laptop (AMD iGPU + NVIDIA dGPU)
2. Install nvidia-open driver (595.58.03)
3. Use Proton / GE-Proton / Proton-CachyOS (Optionally, I tested it with all of these versions & Proton-CachyOS + with VKD3D-Proton (latest upstream git))
4. Launch a DX12-only game, e.g. Cyberpunk 2077
5. Observe Vulkan device creation failure on NVIDIA GPU
[...]
- Set VKD3D_NO_FALLBACK=1 to prevent fallback to iGPU
- Game fails immediately during initialization OR Game launches but only utilizes iGPU.
[...]
Expected behavior:

- Vulkan device creation should succeed on the NVIDIA dGPU, allowing D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton) to run, as it does with the proprietary driver

summary.text:
# NVIDIA Driver Support on Arch Linux: RTX 2070 (Turing) & Driver Choice

## Key Findings

### Hardware Support Status
**RTX 2070 (Turing architecture) is supported** by both:
- `nvidia-open` (open kernel modules) — **Arch's new default**
- `nvidia-580xx-dkms` (proprietary driver) — **Available from AUR**

### Arch Linux's Driver Transition (Driver 590+)
Arch moved to NVIDIA driver 590 with a major shift in package handling:
- Turing (RTX 20xx) and newer → transitioned to **`nvidia-open`** (open kernel modules)
- Pascal (GTX 10xx) and older → dropped from main repos, legacy drivers pushed to AUR
- The package `nvidia` was replaced with `nvidia-open`; `nvidia-dkms` with `nvidia-open-dkms`

### Installing Proprietary Driver Instead of nvidia-open
If you prefer the proprietary driver despite Arch's default:

1. **Uninstall open modules**: Remove `nvidia-open`, `nvidia-open-dkms`, or similar
2. **Install from AUR**: Use `paru` or `yay` to install `nvidia-580xx-dkms` and related utilities:
   ```
   paru -Syu nvidia-580xx-dkms nvidia-580xx-utils lib32-nvidia-580xx-utils
   ```
3. **Optional**: Search AUR for `580xx` to find matching packages (settings, OpenCL, etc.)

No uninstall is strictly required if you simply install the AUR packages on top.

## Known Issues & Caveats

### nvidia-open Specific Issues
- **Hybrid laptop GPUs**: `nvidia-open` fails to create Vulkan devices for D3D12 workloads (VKD3D-Proton), while the proprietary driver works fine
- **GSP Firmware problems**: Can cause poor power management on Turing GPUs and complete failures on Ampere laptops; workaround: use proprietary driver with `NVreg_EnableGpuFirmware=0`
- **Linux 5.18+ with Intel 11th Gen CPUs**: Indirect Branch Tracking (IBT) incompatibility; disable with `ibt=off` kernel parameter (security tradeoff)

### Gaming/VKD3D-Proton Issues (Both Drivers)
- Multiple reports of **VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST crashes** in Cyberpunk 2077 on both NVIDIA 580.x and 595.x drivers
- Crashes appear related to descriptor heap pressure and view map recreation
- Workarounds tested without full success; newer Proton versions sometimes resolve issues

## Current Driver Versions (2026)
- Latest `nvidia-open`: **595.58.03** (as of April 2026)
- Legacy proprietary (`nvidia-580xx-dkms`): **580.119.02** available from AUR

## Sources
1. ArchWiki NVIDIA — https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA
2. NVIDIA Driver 590 Drops Pascal Support: Arch Linux Update Guide — https://ostechnix.com/nvidia-590-driver-arch-linux-manual-intervention/
3. Arch Linux nvidia-open package page — https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/x86_64/nvidia/
4. nvidia-open: vkCreateDevice fails on hybrid laptops — https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules/issues/1123
5. VK_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST Cyberpunk 2077 issues — https://github.com/HansKristian-Work/vkd3d-proton/issues/2756

content.text:
aur/nvidia-580xx-dkms 580.159.04-1 [+78 ~12.49]
    NVIDIA kernel modules - module sources (580xx)

OK, here's the situation. Let me be straight with you:

## Proprietary driver swap is **not** viable

The only proprietary option is `nvidia-580xx-dkms` from AUR, which is **driver 580.x** — two major versions behind your current 595.71.05. Downgrading from 595 to 580 on a kernel 7.0 system is likely to cause DKMS build failures, and you'd also need matching `nvidia-580xx-utils`, `lib32-nvidia-580xx-utils`, etc. It's a mess and could bork your system.

Arch has made the open kernel module the only official path for Turing+ GPUs.

## What we actually can try

Since we're stuck with nvidia-open, let's work the VKD3D side harder.
